EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge during the end of July and beginning August helped fuel a moist and unstable air mass over the region. The ridge broke down on the 1st of August and was overrun by two weak midlevel impulses on August 2nd. As the second impulse passed over Northeastern Washington and Northern Idaho strong storms developed.  Meanwhile, dewpoint depressions over Northern Idaho had spread between 40 and 50 degrees Fahrenheit. The rain and hail cooled outflow winds from storms over Northeastern Washington and North Idaho put out damaging outflow winds which moved northwest to southeast and gained momentum while tracking off the Selkirk Mountain Range.  Winds ranging between 50 and 70 miles per hour blew through Priest Lake, Sandpoint, Lake Pend Oreille, and Prichard.  Meanwhile, isolated thunderstorms were developing along a decaying outflow boundary tracking west to east across the Washington, Idaho border, each producing local tree damage.  Priest Lake, Sandpoint, and communities surrounding Lake Pend Oreille were the hardest hit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coeur D Alene Press reported that thunderstorms brought high winds and downed trees across portions of Post Falls, Hayden, Spirit Lake, and northern Coeur D'Alene.  The Stateline Speedway and several other businesses on Pleasant View Road lost power and were forced to cancel the Idaho 200 race.  The storm and strong winds also sparked a quick grass fire causing Love's Travel Center to evacuate.  The storm was also blamed for a house fire on Cimmaron Street in Post Falls and another one at Fir and 13th Street caused by downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure over the region for the last few weeks was nudged east by a potent upper-level trough coming off the Eastern Pacific. The trough took on a strong negative tilt and passed through during peak heating. An east to west line of strong thunderstorms developed over Northern Oregon and tracked northward through Eastern Washington and Northern Idaho during the late afternoon and early evening. Additional storms continued to develop along the gust front which continued to pickup momentum as it passed into the region into an inverted V environment. Winds between 50 and 60 mph resulted in downed trees and power outages.  The strongest winds were observed between Latah and southern Bonner County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on a power line in Sandpoint, ID.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning sparked wildfire twenty four miles south of Lewiston Idaho torched 65,200 acres of rough terrain west of Wapshilla Ridge and east of the Snake River in southern Nez Perce county.  Nine homes were lost in the fire and three firefighters were injured.  The fire burned through fuels consisting of timber with grass and understory.  The blaze was 100 percent contained on August 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three firefighters were injured fighting the blaze.  One firefighter received a three inch gash on his right leg from a chain saw and required stitches.  Another firefighter suffered heat related problems and a third sustained a scratched cornea.  Two hundred homes were threatened from the fire with only nine structures lost, four which were cabins along the Snake River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure dropped down the Washington Coastline then swung inland into the Inland Northwest during the afternoon of July 23rd. The wave took on a strong negative tilt and moved into a moist and very unstable air mass. The outcome was a widespread severe weather event for Eastern Washington and Northern Idaho producing large hail, flash flooding, and widespread wind damage. Falling trees led to some injuries in Eastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four cows and one calf were killed by a lightning strike in Hayden, ID.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rains caused flash flooding in Power, Bingham, Jefferson, and Bannock Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of rain in less than an hour caused extensive street flooding in Chubbuck and Pocatello.    Several inches reported at intersection of Yellowstone and Flandro roads in Pocatello.   Standing water was reported at the intersection of Yellowstone Road and Cedar.   The Chubbuck WalMart reported flooding inside the building with damage to merchandise.  Basement flooding was reported at the intersection of Hawthorne and Homestead Roads.   High Estates Trailer Park on Philbin Road in Pocatello was flooded with several inches of water.   Hawthorne Road at the interstate 86 underpass in Chubbuck was closed due to extensive standing water.   Garrett Way in Pocatello near Alameda was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second consecutive day of heavy rainfall in southeastern Idaho caused flash flooding mainly in Blaine, Power, and Bingham Counties.   The worst of the flooding occurred in the city of Blackfoot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly 2 inches of rain in less than an hour brought extensive flooding to the city of Blackfoot.   Extensive street and parking lot flooding occurred with standing water on roadways a problem throughout the city.   Several cars stalled out driving through water with one truck driving into an uncovered manhole   Several manhole covers in roads became uncovered as the amount of water was too much for the cities drainage system.   30 homes and 15 businesses reported damage with house basements receiving the most damage.   Several roads were also damaged.   Damage in Blackfoot was estimated at $300,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roberts ARL mesnonet site recorded 60 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought significant snowfall and difficult travel conditions to all of southeast Idaho over the Christmas holiday.   Several traffic accidents occurred with two fatalities occurring on interstate 15 just south of Pocatello.  6 to 20 inch snowfall amounts were common throughout the region making holiday travel extremely difficult.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm dropped 6 to 8 inches of snow in the cities of Chubbuck and Pocatello with 8 to 10 inches on the Pocatello east and west benches.   A fatal traffic accident occurred on interstate 15 in south Pocatello in which a mother and her daughter died with injuries to 5 other children in the vehicle.   The accident occurred around 11 am and closed interstate 15 in both directions for nearly two hours.  Another accident on interstate 15 in Pocatello occurred around 5 pm with three injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system pushing into the region off of the Pacific brought heavy snow across the Northern Panhandle valleys and mountains.  Strong low level warm air advection limited heavy snowfall above 3,000 feet in elevation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations ranged between two and six inches in the valleys with ten inches reported at Schweitzer Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of Pacific moisture brought heavy snow to the mountains and moderate to heavy rain to the valleys across the area overnight on January 10th into the 11th.  Localized flooding was observed in the Northern Panhandle due to a combination of the rainfall, snow melt and frozen soils.  Rainfall amounts were generally up into the half inch to one inch range for this period, which typically would not cause flooding if not in combination with low elevation snow melt and frozen soils preventing water from seeping into the ground.  Flooding impacts were minor and limited to low spots in the terrain and along small streams.  Rain and heavy snow was followed by a strong cold front passage during the early morning hours of January 11th with strong and damaging winds observed across the much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fleming Creek Road washed out in two locations and was closed off.  Little damage was reported along the side of the road and was reopened the next day.  Sandbags were placed around a house on Moon Shadow Road due to some basement flooding.  Damage to the home was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system pushing into the region off of the Pacific brought heavy snow across the Northern Panhandle valleys and mountains.  Strong low level warm air advection limited heavy snowfall above 3,000 feet in elevation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall of nine inches reported at Lookout Pass Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of shortwaves diving south from Canada dropped into the Inland Northwest between the second and third of February.  The first wave generated light to moderate snow across the Idaho Panhandle driven by warm advection processes.  Behind the first wave, much colder and unstable air descended into the region.  Forcing associated with a second shortwave resulted in small scale but moderate to heavy convective snow showers.  This resulted in two to four inches of snow across a majority of the Idaho Panhandle.  Upwards of four to six inches fell across the Central Idaho Panhandle and Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged between two and four inches with local amounts to five inches which led to delays, accidents, and disruptions to travel during the Monday morning commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This period was dominated by a strong upper level ridge of high pressure. Subsidence associated with this ridge trapped low level moisture with widespread fog developing across the area. Freezing temperatures and dense fog produced some rime on power lines leading to power outages across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Avista Utility lost a transmission line, a broken conductor and broken poles located between Reardan to the Washington state border due to ice buildup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of Pacific moisture brought heavy snow to the mountains and moderate to heavy rain to the valleys across the area overnight on January 10th into the 11th.  Localized flooding was observed in the Northern Panhandle due to a combination of the rainfall, snow melt and frozen soils.  Rainfall amounts were generally up into the half inch to one inch range for this period, which typically would not cause flooding if not in combination with low elevation snow melt and frozen soils preventing water from seeping into the ground.  Flooding impacts were minor and limited to low spots in the terrain and along small streams.  Rain and heavy snow was followed by a strong cold front passage during the early morning hours of January 11th with strong and damaging winds observed across the much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 to 50 miles per hour with gusts between 70 to 90 miles per hour observed at the Lakeview sensor on Schweitzer Mountain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed along Highway 3 at milepost 102.5 and partially blocked the road.  A blown tree  knocked out power to  2,000 Avista Utility customers in the Coeur d'Alene area.  Another tree fell over onto some power lines in Hayden, which knocked out the power to 2,000 customers in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening shortwave trough of low pressure spun up a low pressure system at the surface.  This low pressure system swung through a strong warm front that tapped into an atmospheric river with sub-tropic origins out of the Pacific Ocean.  This moist warm front produced widespread heavy wet snowfall across the Central Panhandle Mountains, Coeur d'Alene area and Northern Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous snowfall reports of between three and seven inches and as much as ten inches reported in the valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed mid-level low tracked from southwest Idaho on Nov. 1 to near Cut Bank by early morning on Nov. 2. While this happened, heavy snow developed along and to the northwest of this feature in the mountains with snow levels generally above 4,000 feet. This was the first significant widespread mountain snowfall for the season, with many hunters and back-country users still in the high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anywhere from 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across the higher elevations of western Lemhi County during this event. The heaviest fell with the frontal passage in the late evening hours potentially catching any back-country person off-guard.

CZ_NAME: LOWER HELLS CANYON / SALMON RIVER REGION
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass had begun to filter into central Idaho on Nov. 10 with highs only reaching near freezing in Grangeville, 26 F on 11/11 and 25 F on 11/12. Relatively mild and moist flow developed on Nov. 13 which caused overrunning snowfall to occur. Due to morning sunshine, road surface temperatures rose to above freezing causing the initial snowfall to melt and re-freeze. Dry snow fell on top of this ice layer leading to very slick conditions. Also this was the first measurable snowfall for this area for the season, which could have caught some people off-guard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the first measurable snowfall for central Idaho for the 2014/2015 winter season. According to road sensors at Whitebird Grade, road temperatures were above freezing before snow began to fall after 12 pm PST. This contributed to a flash freeze scenario causing extremely slick conditions. A snow plow struck a sheriff's cruiser around 1500 PST which caused Whitebird Grade to close for several hours, opening up around 1745 PST. The Idaho State Police reported multiple semi-trucks that had jack-knifed and 2 vehicle slide-offs. These impacts, along with the fact that this was the first winter weather for the area, justified the classification as a high impact event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving through the Great Basin spread snow and pockets of freezing rain across parts of Southwest Idaho and Southeast Oregon making roads slick and hazardous. Numerous accidents were reported including 3 fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two fatalities occurred in Jerome County in two separate incidents in the early morning due to snow covered roads. The first accident occurred at 5:37 AM on Interstate 84 near mile marker 179 when the vehicle slid into the median and rolled over. The second occurred at 6:06 AM on Interstate 84 near mile marker 170. The vehicle overturned after hitting an icy patch on an overpass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused blowing dust and zero visibility on interstate 15 north of Idaho Falls near Roberts.   A nine vehicle crash occurred at around 240 pm south of Roberts.  The dust storm and near zero visibility occurred from around noon through 830 pm.   Interstate 15 closed for 7 hours after the crash between mile posts 119 and 135 in both directions.  Ten people were transported to the Eastern Idaho Regional Medical Center for treatment.

Ten people were transported to the Eastern Idaho Regional Medical Center for treatment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over North Idaho on the 25th and 26th of November triggered a debris flow which covered the Burlington Northern Santa Fe railroad line near the Idaho and Montana border 14 miles east of Bonners Ferry, Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KHQ TV reported a mudslide 14 miles east of Bonners Ferry, Idaho which covered the BNSF railroad tracks. Slide was 20 feet deep, 50 feet wide, and 200 feet long. The slide halted train traffic for 48 hours until it was cleared.

Snow amounts of 3 to 7 inches were common in the Snake River Plain and Eastern Magic Valley with over a foot common in high elevations of the southern and eastern Highlands as well as Central Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to 6 inches of snow fell in the Lower Snake River Plain on the 13th and 14th with the main issue several traffic accidents with extensive damage occurring.   Many of the accidents occurred on interstates.  Around noon a major accident occurred on interstate 15 south of Blackfoot with another near 1 pm in south Pocatello trapping a motorist.   At 245 pm another accident on interstate 15 south of Pocatello again.   Two different rollovers occurred on interstate 86 in Power county.  One was near Rainbow Road and the other near the Pocatello Regional Airport.  Bingham County Sheriff reported several accidents on interstate 15 between Blackfoot and fort Hall with five injuries.

Snow amounts of 3 to 7 inches were common in the Snake River Plain and Eastern Magic Valley with over a foot common in high elevations of the southern and eastern Highlands as well as Central Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two day dnowfall totals were the following at these SNOTEL sites:  Chocolate Gulch 9 inches, Dollarhide Summit 19 inches, Galena 15 inches, Galena Summit 16 inches, Lost Wood Divide 14 inches, Vienna Mine 29 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several traffic accidents occurred on February 3rd with one fatality on interstate 15 and heavy snow fell in the Upper Snake River Highlands.   Several roads closed the evening of the 3rd including parts of interstate 15, US Route 20 and State Highway 87.

Ashton reported 6 inches of snow and snow, blowing snow and reduced visibilities caused the closing it interstate 15 from Dubois to the Montana state line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although snow amounts were only in the 1 to 3 inch range roadways became very difficult after sunset with several accidents in the Idaho Falls area.   One crash on interstate 15 north of the Dad's truck stop in Idaho falls caused a fatality.

A large tree was felled into a cable line in Pocatello on West Sherman Street.   2,800 Idaho Power customers were without power in Chubbuck and Pocatello for a period of time around noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain turned to snow behind the passage of a cold front the morning of the 30th.   Snow amounts in the Snake River Plain and Caribou Highlands ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific jet oriented west to east across central Oregon kept an active storm track along the Washington, Oregon border. The second low pressure system in as many days took aim on the lower Idaho Panhandle during the overnight hours of the 8th. A band of moderate to heavy snow on the northeastern periphery of the low dumped upwards of four to six inches of snow across the Idaho Palouse. Meanwhile, northeast winds drawn into the system continued to draw cold, continental air in from Canada. The combination of temperatures in the teens to lower twenties and northeast winds between 15 and 25 mph resulted in areas of blowing snow and drifts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from three to five inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river overrunning a dome of cold air established over the Idaho Panhandle resulted in an extended period of moderate to heavy precipitation across the region.  Precipitation mainly fell in the form of snow in the mountains of the Idaho Panhandle resulting in snow accumulations of seven to twelve inches.  In the valleys, snow gradually transitioned to rain after four to six inches of accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A solid four to six inches of snow fell across northern Idaho.  The most snow fell in the community of Colburn which reported six inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure dropped down the Washington Coastline then swung inland into the Inland Northwest during the afternoon of July 23rd. The wave took on a strong negative tilt and moved into a moist and very unstable air mass. The outcome was a widespread severe weather event for Eastern Washington and Northern Idaho producing large hail, flash flooding, and widespread wind damage. Falling trees led to some injuries in Eastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds led to widespread tree damage across central Bonner County.  A navy bouy located on the south end of Lake Pend Oreille clocked a wind gust of 75 mph.  Multiple trees were knocked down across the city of Sandpoint and smaller communities surrounding Lake Pend Oreille.  Many roads were closed until trees and power lines were removed including Highway 95 near the intersection of McArthur Street.  Power was out for the entire city of Sandpoint and majority of Bonner County directly following the storm.  Several trees fell on houses.  Southern exposed bays on Lake Pend Oreille experienced the worst damage.  Over thirty trees were knocked down between Gamlin Lake and Garfield Bay.  Two people were injured by a falling tree at Green Bay.  Further north on the lake, roughly twenty trees were downed in Shaw Bay.  On a ridge, known as Hawkins Point, over fifty trees were snapped four feet from the ground.  The winds created three to six foot waves in sections of the lake resulting in dock and boat damage.  Broadcast media also reported numerous trees downed at the Western Pleasure Guest Ranch located eleven miles northeast of Ponderay.

Falling trees led to some injuries in Eastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spruce and locus tree were destroyed around a farm house.  Two roofs on farm buildings lost tin.  Tree limbs four inches in diameter were broken off into four foot chunks and flew 100 feet.  Fifty to sixty foot spruce trees with six inch diameter trunks had twenty feet of their tops taken off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed across the Camas Prairie and peaked in intensity over western Clearwater County between Greer and Weippe. The storm unleashed severe winds and half-dollar hail causing close to a million dollars in property and crop damage. The U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) designated Clearwater County as a primary natural disaster area due to this wind and hail event on Sep. 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A law enforcement office reported mostly 1 hail but a few were up to half-dollar size. The hail accumulated up to 12 inches in places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave initiated a thunderstorm that produced very heavy rainfall and subsequently a debris flow along Magruder Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy thunderstorm produced heavy rains and severe mudslides along the Magruder Corridor mainly between Scimitar Creek and the CCC Camp. There were about 6 washouts reported with large trees and rock debris making the road impassable. Timing was based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge during the end of July and beginning August helped fuel a moist and unstable air mass over the region. The ridge broke down on the 1st of August and was overrun by two weak midlevel impulses on August 2nd. As the second impulse passed over Northeastern Washington and Northern Idaho strong storms developed.  Meanwhile, dewpoint depressions over Northern Idaho had spread between 40 and 50 degrees Fahrenheit. The rain and hail cooled outflow winds from storms over Northeastern Washington and North Idaho put out damaging outflow winds which moved northwest to southeast and gained momentum while tracking off the Selkirk Mountain Range.  Winds ranging between 50 and 70 miles per hour blew through Priest Lake, Sandpoint, Lake Pend Oreille, and Prichard.  Meanwhile, isolated thunderstorms were developing along a decaying outflow boundary tracking west to east across the Washington, Idaho border, each producing local tree damage.  Priest Lake, Sandpoint, and communities surrounding Lake Pend Oreille were the hardest hit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed along Coeur D Alene River Road between mile posts twenty and twenty-eight.  Eighteen trees downed on Miller Way in Prichard ranging in height between 75 and 105 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lewiston ASOS measured a peak gust of 62 mph.  Lewiston Tribune reported that the Lewiston Orchards experienced some minor flooding of roads and residential areas and some limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed across the Camas Prairie and peaked in intensity over western Clearwater County between Greer and Weippe. The storm unleashed severe winds and half-dollar hail causing close to a million dollars in property and crop damage. The U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) designated Clearwater County as a primary natural disaster area due to this wind and hail event on Sep. 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farmer reported major property and crop damage due to both large hail and wind. The property damage included 5 rental home roofs, siding from 3 homes, several windows, and 12 garage doors that either were dented or destroyed. Approximately 75% of his spring wheat crop, approximately 450 acres, was decimated by the wind and hail. In the 67 years that he has lived there, there was only one other storm that was comparable to this one. Back in 1963, a similar storm decimated his dad's crops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USDA reported that at least 400 acres of hard red spring wheat were destroyed and 700 acres were damaged.  They also reported that 183.6 acres of soft wheat were destroyed and 189.2 acres were damaged. All in all, the total crop damage tallied at $425,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave tracked from southern Idaho to western Montana initiating thunderstorm activity. Due to an above normal amount of atmospheric moisture, the embedded thunderstorms brought very heavy rainfall triggering debris flows across the Mustang burn scar west of Shoup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 20 ponderosa pine trees fell down onto the main Salmon River Road while one pine tree smashed the USGS river gauge on the Salmon River.  Evidence revealed that debris flows occurred at Boulder Creek, Owl Creek, Colson Creek and at an unnamed gulch. The unnamed gulch produced a large debris flow with rock onto the main Salmon River Road. A man-made dam located near the delta of Colson Creek was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped anywhere from 1 to 3 inches in the city of Rexburg.  A single thunderstorm cell moved over the city and heavy rain began at around 5:30 pm MDT and brought the 1 to 3 inch rainfall.   Main Street was shut down with over a foot of running water on it.  Businesses on Main Street suffered damage.   The Hidden Valley neighborhood had basements with 5 feet of water.   The BYU Idaho campus was hit hard by the flooding.    Apartments on campus had 6 feet of standing water in them.   Birch Plaza and Birchwood Apartments were the worst hit on campus.  Water was high enough there that furniture was floating.   The Madison County Annex and Romance Theater on Main Street suffered damage.   And estimated 20 to 30 residential homes suffered serious flooding.    Crops were damaged as well due to the flooding in Rexburg.    Total damages of the flooding were estimated at $600,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped anywhere from 1 to 3 inches in the city of Rexburg.  A single thunderstorm cell moved over the city and heavy rain began at around 5:30 pm MDT.  Main Street was shut down with over a foot of running water on it.  Businesses on Main Street suffered damage.   15 businesses on the south side of Main Street and south 2nd West received minor flood damage consisting of a few inches of water entering their businesses.   ||The Hidden Valley neighborhood in northeast Rexburg had approximately 20 homes from a few inches in a basement up to a basement full in 2 of them.   The Mill Hollow  subdivision had 20 homes reported having water damage.  A few homes had 1 to 3 feet of water in their basements.  ||The BYU Idaho campus was hit hard by the flooding.    Three-quarters of the buildings on campus had water in them.   Apartments on campus had up to 6 feet of standing water in them.   Birch Plaza and Birchwood Apartments were the worst hit on campus.  Water was high enough that furniture was floating in the buildings.   ||Total damages of the flooding were estimated at $600,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed mid-level low tracked from southwest Idaho on Nov. 1 to near Cut Bank by early morning on Nov. 2. While this happened, heavy snow developed along and to the northwest of this feature in the mountains with snow levels generally above 4,000 feet. This was the first significant widespread mountain snowfall for the season, with many hunters and back-country users still in the high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public reported up to 5 inches of snow in the higher terrain in the vicinity of Carmen Creek area. According to nearby sensors, this snow fell in a short amount of time associated with the cold front passage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain turned to snow behind the passage of a cold front the morning of the 30th.   Snow amounts in the Snake River Plain and Caribou Highlands ranged from 6 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow fell at the Pebble Creek Ski Resort.   6 inches of snow fell in Grace.  5 inches fell at the Pine Creek Pass SNOTEL with 6 inches at the Wild Horse Divide SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain turned to snow behind the passage of a cold front the morning of the 30th.   Snow amounts in the Snake River Plain and Caribou Highlands ranged from 6 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow fell through the morning on the 30th behind a cold front.   Blackfoot reported 6 inches of snow with 5.3 inches at the Pocatello National Weather Service Office and 5 inches in Chubbuck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in high elevations of the Upper Snake River Highlands.   SNOTEL amounts were 9 inches at White Elephant and 13 inches at Phillips Bench.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in high elevations of the Upper Snake River Highlands.   SNOTEL amounts were 9 inches at White Elephant and 13 inches at Phillips Bench.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent snowfall brings slick roads at times across central Idaho due to moist Pacific flow overrunning the cooler air mass at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP located one mile north of Dixie measured a three day total snowfall of 16.9 with 1.21 of precipitation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CoCoRaHS observer located 0.4 southwest of Grangeville reported a four day storm snow total of 11.6 with 1.31 of precipitation. Other snowfall amounts include: 6 at the Fenn Ranger Station and 8 two miles west-southwest of Cottonwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent snowfall brings slick roads at times across central Idaho due to moist Pacific flow overrunning the cooler air mass at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP located one mile south-southeast of Headquarters reported a three day snowfall total of 13.1 with 1.57 of precipitation. The COOP in Pierce observed 8.6 of snowfall with 1.75 of precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm began on February 27th but brunt of it occurred on March 1st.   Storm brought heavy snow to the southeast Idaho mountains with blizzard conditions and road closures in the Upper Snake River Highlands on March 1st.   Many SNOTEL sites recorded over 1 foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were reported on the morning of the 1st with northerly wind gusts of 35 to 60 mph reported.   US Highway 20 was closed due to low visibility in heavy snow and blowing snow from Ashton through Island Park to the Montana border.   Some motorists on the road were stranded for 18 hours and were delivered food and water on emergency snowmobiles.   4 inches fell in Victor from midnight through 7 am on the 1st.   Total amounts from the 27th through the early morning hours on the 2nd at SNOTEL sites were:  19 inches at Phillips Bench and 13 inches at White Elephant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was under a cold and dry air mass as the calendar flipped over to March 1st with an Arctic boundary pushing south of central Idaho. Drier northerly flow flipped over to a moister and mild southerly flow by March 2nd as a sub-tropical plume of moisture impacted the region.  Strong isentropic ascent in the warm sector of the storm system produced heavy snowfall across the valleys of the Central Panhandle, Coeur d'Alene Area and the Northern Panhandle.

A warm front generally brought anywhere from 0.25 to 0.50 of precipitation to central Idaho. Arctic air was entrenched in the region which allowed for difficult road mitigation due to very cold temperatures and allowed for snow to liquid ratios to be near 20 to 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts in the Grangeville area ranged from 4 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was under a transitioning weather regime through the first week of March. March 1st came in dry and very cold under a modified Arctic air mass. This kept soil temperatures below freezing across much of the central to northern Idaho Panhandle. March 2nd into the 3rd brought heavy snow across these same areas as dry northerly flow was replaced by milder and wetter southerly flow. This wetter and milder flow regime continued into March 5th, but with snow levels lifting above valleys floors. A pair of Pacific storm systems bringing through more sub-tropical moisture into the region resulted in moderate to heavy rainfall in the valleys. The combination of frozen soils, heavy rain and snow melt produced flooding across portions of the Coeur d'Alene Area and Northern Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large road washout ten feet across and eight feet deep on Rimrock Road due to a failed culvert.  Two cars drove into a ditch resulting in three injuries.  The washout also resulted in a loss of water to 150 residences in the area.  Damage costs were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system in the Gulf of Alaska late on March 8th through the evening of March 9th directed a slow north to south sagging atmospheric river across the area.  The origins of this atmospheric river were from off of the Islands of Hawaii.  This resulted in abnormally high moisture content over the region for early to mid-March.  This was a very mild weather pattern with precipitation falling as rain even over the highest mountain peaks.  Strong westerly flow resulted in strong orographic enhancement across the mountainous areas of the northern and central Idaho Panhandle.  This was combined with good isentropic ascent to produce moderate to heavy rainfall across the area.  Mild temperatures resulted in a significant contribution of runoff from the snow pack at mid elevations.  This resulted in high flows on the Coeur d'Alene River and along small streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water reported across the area.  A mobile home park in Ponderay was hardest hit with one trailer inundated with two feet of water inside the home.  Damage costs were estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public located one mile north of Dixie estimated a storm total snowfall of 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level systems, one of which was connected to a moisture plume with subtropical origins, moved across the region and brought rain and snow to north-central Idaho. A warm occlusion brought a transition from snow to rain during the morning hours of Feb. 12 to elevations below 7,000 feet. The combination of strong warming aloft with strong winds most likely nixed the ability for the formation of stellar dendrites; therefore, heavy snow wasn't able to materialize in the mountain pass areas most commonly traveled. Nonetheless, moderate impact was experienced during the nighttime and morning hours as light to moderate snow made road surfaces slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lost Trail Pass received around 4 of total snowfall while with a total precipitation around 1.00 according to the Saddle Mountain SNOTEL. Light snow fell in the Salmon area on Feb. 12 with one inch of total snow reported. Williams Creek saw 5.5 of snow through 8:53 am MST on Feb. 12. A weather spotter located one mile north-northwest of Salmon reported snow at 4:30 am MST on Feb. 13 that turned to freezing rain before 7 am. They reported that sidewalks and other surfaces were slick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous occluded front pushed across the region through the early afternoon hours.  Steepening low to mid-level lapse rates resulted in destabilizing atmosphere for the afternoon due to warming at the surface from the sun and cooling aloft by the approaching upper level shortwave trough.  Forcing along the upper level wave produced a squall line that pushed into the central Idaho Panhandle.  The squall line produced strong wind gusts down to the surface resulting in isolated straight lined wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree toppled over onto power lines and across two lanes along Highway 5 near St. Maries.  Damage costs estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Transportation Department reported that many snow slides had blocked US-93 south of Lost Trail Pass. One of the avalanches was reportedly 700 feet in length.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph and wind gusts of 55 to 75 mph occurred on March 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts from 55 to 75 mph occurred throughout the Eastern Magic Valley.   The winds caused a semi-truck to flip around 1:30 PM on Highway 25 near milepost 55 near Paul.   There were some power outages in Paul, Rupert, and Richfield.   There was also reported some minor roof and shingle damage in Rupert and Declo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper level low centered over west-central Washington State at 6 am MDT on July 14 slowly moved eastward across western Montana by 6 am on July 15. Due to the above normal amounts of atmospheric moisture with origins from the sub-tropics, the thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall in a short amount of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter at the Leadore Ranger Station reported dime to penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge during the end of July and beginning August helped fuel a moist and unstable air mass over the region. The ridge broke down on the 1st of August and was overrun by two weak midlevel impulses on August 2nd. As the second impulse passed over Northeastern Washington and Northern Idaho strong storms developed.  Meanwhile, dewpoint depressions over Northern Idaho had spread between 40 and 50 degrees Fahrenheit. The rain and hail cooled outflow winds from storms over Northeastern Washington and North Idaho put out damaging outflow winds which moved northwest to southeast and gained momentum while tracking off the Selkirk Mountain Range.  Winds ranging between 50 and 70 miles per hour blew through Priest Lake, Sandpoint, Lake Pend Oreille, and Prichard.  Meanwhile, isolated thunderstorms were developing along a decaying outflow boundary tracking west to east across the Washington, Idaho border, each producing local tree damage.  Priest Lake, Sandpoint, and communities surrounding Lake Pend Oreille were the hardest hit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage was observed across the cities of Priest Lake, Sandpoint, Ponderay, Kootenai, Oden, and Lake Pend Oreille.  Hundreds of trees ranging from eight to twenty two inches in diameter were snapped or downed.  Some trees landed on homes, others took out miles of power lines and poles.  At one point after the storm, all of Sandpoint, Idaho was without power.  A spotter in Clark Fork stated fifteen foot trees were almost touching the ground while sixty foot trees were bending at 45 degree angles.  The most widespread damage was along the Highway 200 corridor between Hope and Sandpoint.  The Elk Golf Course in this corridor lost thirteen trees.  The Sandpoint AWOS, also in this area, measured sustained winds of 40 mph with gusts to 56 mph before the power outage.  A roof of a storage shed was blown off in downtown Sandpoint.  Numerous boats and docks on Lake Pend Oreille were damaged from waves that ranged between three to six feet.  Significant tree damage was observed on the south side of the lake in Bottle Bay between the 3800th and 5016th blocks.  All trees were leaning to the east southeast.  Residents interviewed during a storm survey mentioned the winds lasted roughly 40 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fill in later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on MCatty Road.  Heavy rain accompanied the storm causing small rocks and mud to flow onto Thunder Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific jet oriented west to east across central Oregon kept an active storm track along the Washington, Oregon border. The second low pressure system in as many days took aim on the lower Idaho Panhandle during the overnight hours of the 8th. A band of moderate to heavy snow on the northeastern periphery of the low dumped upwards of four to six inches of snow across the Idaho Palouse. Meanwhile, northeast winds drawn into the system continued to draw cold, continental air in from Canada. The combination of temperatures in the teens to lower twenties and northeast winds between 15 and 25 mph resulted in areas of blowing snow and drifts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from three to five inches.  The official snow observer from Lewiston reported five inches while a CoCoRaHs observer located 2.6 miles southeast of Lewiston reported four inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather pattern across the Eastern Pacific led to region-wide light to moderate snow.  A closed upper-level high was positioned over southeastern Alaska. The high steered modified Arctic air southward into the Inland Northwest. Meanwhile, a moist westerly jet undercut the Alaskan high bringing a source of moisture into the region. A combination of a stalled frontal boundary, topography, and isentropic processes resulted in region-wide snow. The heaviest snow was delivered in two waves; the first occurred from the evening hours of the 22nd through the evening of the 23rd with a second surge of moderate to heavy snow during the day on the 24th. The heaviest snow accumulations were concentrated across the Central Panhandle Mountains of Idaho which received between one to three feet of snow. Nearly all other valley locations received between four to ten inches over the multi-day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts over the two day period ranged between six and twelve inches. The first round of snow which fell on the 23rd brought three to six inches.  A second round of snow on the 24th brought an additional three to six inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over a foot of snow fell across the higher terrain of north-central Idaho as an upper level trough laden with Pacific moisture moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was one tree of unknown size that fell partly onto a house and gutters were knocked partially off of homes in Grangeville. The peak wind gust measured was from the NWS handar located one mile north of Grangeville, at the Grangeville Airport. Strong gusty winds caused a downed power line and started a fire in the Clearwater area of Idaho County around 5:12 am PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over a foot of snow fell across the higher terrain of north-central Idaho as an upper level trough laden with Pacific moisture moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first of two avalanches near mile-marker 345 on US-93 that crossed the highway located just south of Lost Trail Pass, was 900 feet in length, 24 feet wide and 6 to 8 feet deep. A second avalanche occurred approximately 18 minutes later at 7:30 am MST at the same location and was 100 feet in length, 12 feet side, and 6 to 8 feet deep. One lane of US-93 was opened up by 8:30 am MST and by 10 am MST all lanes were open. No injuries or vehicle damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of shortwaves diving south from Canada dropped into the Inland Northwest between the second and third of February.  The first wave generated light to moderate snow across the Idaho Panhandle driven by warm advection processes.  Behind the first wave, much colder and unstable air descended into the region.  Forcing associated with a second shortwave resulted in small scale but moderate to heavy convective snow showers.  This resulted in two to four inches of snow across a majority of the Idaho Panhandle.  Upwards of four to six inches fell across the Central Idaho Panhandle and Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from two to four inches leading to delays and disruptions during the Monday morning commute.  Two children died Sunday, February 2nd after falling through ice covering a pond near Plummer.  The children were looking for their dog and it is unknown if the children were unaware of the pond which was blanketed with light snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic air mass spilled into the Inland Northwest between the 4th and 5th of February. The combination of single digit temperatures and winds on the order of 10 to 25 mph with gusts in excess of 40 mph created wind chills between minus fifteen and minus twenty-five degrees Fahrenheit in the valleys and up to minus thirty degrees Fahrenheit in the mountains on the morning of the 5th.  Bitterly cold wind chills continued during the morning of the 6th but were paired with slightly cooler temperatures and weaker winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dipped to minus 20 degrees Fahrenheit throughout the windier Purcell Trench and between minus 10 and minus 18 in the more sheltered valleys.  The coldest wind chill observed was at Sandpoint which dipped to minus 20 Fahrenheit.  Schools in the Sandpoint district closed due to the cold.

The coldest day for Grangeville was Feb. 6 when the the high only reached 10 degrees while the low was -13 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved through between Jan. 8-9 and brought snowfall to the Clearwater Mountains. Northwest flow set up by Jan. 10 which triggered moderate to heavy snow bands that were oriented west-northwest to east-southeast. The snow bands brought the heaviest snowfall across eastern portions of both Clearwater and Idaho Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pierce COOP reported 5.5 of snow and 1.28 of precipitation during this event. The Elk Butte SNOTEL at 5,690 ft., located 5 miles northeast of Elk River measured 15 of snow from Jan 7-10.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer located 1 mile north of Dixie measured 8.8 of total storm snowfall with 0.71 of precipitation. The Savage Pass SNOTEL at 6,170 ft., located 5 miles southeast of Powell, recorded 9 of snowfall with 1.10 of snow water equivalent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved through between Jan. 8-9 and brought snowfall to the Clearwater Mountains. Northwest flow set up by Jan. 10 which triggered moderate to heavy snow bands that were oriented west-northwest to east-southeast. The snow bands brought the heaviest snowfall across eastern portions of both Clearwater and Idaho Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grangeville COOP observer reported 1 of total snowfall while the observer located 2 miles west-southwest of Cottonwood recorded 1.2. Web cams at Greer Grade indicated slick conditions at times during the morning of Jan. 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved through between Jan. 8-9 and brought snowfall to the Clearwater Mountains. Northwest flow set up by Jan. 10 which triggered moderate to heavy snow bands that were oriented west-northwest to east-southeast. The snow bands brought the heaviest snowfall across eastern portions of both Clearwater and Idaho Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The total Jan. 9-10 snowfall for Leadore was 6.5 with 0.37 of precipitation. A person from the public in Salmon reported 4 of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northerly flow generated snowfall across the Camas Prairie especially affecting the Grangeville area Feb. 4. Arctic air produced very cold temperatures across the high valleys of Idaho and Lemhi Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer located one mile north of Dixie observed a minimum temperature of -31 deg. F on the morning of Feb. 6. Other cold temperatures observed include: -33 Elk City (ITD highway sensor) and -23 F at Lodgepole (RAWS).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific jet nosing into central Oregon from the northwest drove a cold upper-level low from Canada into the Inland Northwest. Southwest flow ahead of the trough resulted in a period of moist isentropic ascent across the Idaho Panhandle bringing the first round of light snow. Snow intensified overnight on the 6th into the morning of the 7th when a surface low tracked across southeastern Washington setting up a narrow deformation band of moderate to heavy snow. Many locations across the lower Idaho Panhandle received between one and three inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between two and four inches.  The heaviest snowfall was recorded in the city of Moscow where amounts were closer to four inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and conditionally unstable air mass had become established across the Inland Northwest as a cold upper-level trough anchored over the region. A low pressure system tracked across northern Oregon spreading a band of moderate snow within a deformation axis across the lower Idaho Panhandle. As the low pressure system departed to the east, northwest flow in its wake led to numerous snow showers across the rising terrain of the Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged between three and five inches at locations like Nez Perce and Winchester to seven inches at Waha.

A low pressure system tracked across northern Oregon spreading a band of moderate snow within a deformation axis across the lower Idaho Panhandle. As the low pressure system departed to the east, northwest flow in its wake led to numerous snow showers across the rising terrain of the Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the zone ranged from two to three inches with 2.9 inches reported in the city of Lewiston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous areas across the Idaho Panhandle dealt with flooding during the second week of February. While minor flooding is a common occurrence during the late winter months when warmer temperatures, wind, and rain fall on valley snowpack, there was one ingredient of this event that made it unique and rather intense for many locations. This was the intrusion of two Arctic air masses; one in December and one in February. These bitterly cold air masses arrived in the absence of valley snow pack and froze the ground hard and deep. Shortly after the second Arctic air mass, snow began falling across the region with anywhere between six and twelve inches of valley snowfall. Then came an atmospheric river. This drove temperatures and dewpoints above freezing, led to windy conditions, and brought moderate to heavy rainfall. Nearly all the valley snowpack melted in a span of 24 hours.  Due to the rock hard ground, rain and snow melt could not percolate into the frozen ground equating to nearly 100 percent runoff.  This led to big problems in hilly terrain, low spots, and areas susceptible to poor drainage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy runoff led to several drive washouts for residents on hilly terrain across Kootenai County.  A swollen Latour creek washed away a small levee and parcel of land that supported a shed at a private residence on South Latour Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist warm front overrunning an established pool of cold air over Northern Idaho resulted in several hours of light to moderate snowfall on the 10th of February.  The heaviest snow fell along a narrow corridor between Bonner and Benewah Counties where forcing was enhanced along a stationary frontal boundary and rising terrain resulting in several hours of moderate to snowfall.  Accumulations of three to four inches were observed over a span of just a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow brought a quick three to four inches along the Interstate 90 corridor with higher amounts in the higher terrain.  A COOP from Prichard received eight inches of snow.

The heaviest snow fell along a narrow corridor between Bonner and Benewah Counties where forcing was enhanced along a stationary frontal boundary and rising terrain resulting in several hours of moderate to snowfall.  Accumulations of three to four inches were observed over a span of just a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow brought quick accumulations of three to four inches across Bonner County while Boundary county only picked up around two inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet stream combined with above normal Pacific moisture gave the right combination for heavy snowfall across the central Idaho mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lost Trail Ski Resort reported 6 of heavy snow by 10:23 am MST on Jan. 29. The MT DOT reported snow, blowing and drifting snow at Lost Trail during the afternoon and evening hours on Jan. 29. According to the Saddle Mountain SNOTEL located 1 mile west of Lost Trail Pass on US-93, 16 of total snow with 1.90 snow water equivalent fell between Jan. 29-30. Salmon saw 7 of heavy snow that mainly fell on Jan 29. The Salmon School District closed early because of the snow loading concerns with their aging roofs. Other total snowfall amounts include: 6 Carmen and 3 in May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist westerly jet aimed at the Pacific Northwest steered a moist laden frontal system through the region resulting in heavy mountain snow and mix of rain, snow, and windy conditions in the valleys.  Valley locations received between one and five inches of snow.  In the mountains, snow amounts ranged between eight and fourteen inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowplow company on Schweitzer Mountain, located at an elevation of 5000 feet, reported fourteen inches during the early morning hours of the 17th.  Two to three inches of snow fell in lower elevations including Sandpoint and Hope.  Clark Fork reported six inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist westerly jet aimed at the Pacific Northwest steered a moist laden frontal system through the region resulting in heavy mountain snow and mix of rain, snow, and windy conditions in the valleys.  The heaviest snow within the warm sector generally fell above elevations of 3500 feet.  Following a cold front passage, snow levels lowered back to valley floors and post frontal showers brought up to five inches of snow to some valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Schweitzer Mountain Ski Resort received eight inches of snow.  Snowfall amounts in the valleys ranged between one half and three inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist westerly jet aimed at the Pacific Northwest steered a moist laden frontal system through the region resulting in heavy mountain snow and mix of rain, snow, and windy conditions in the valleys.  The heaviest snow within the warm sector generally fell above elevations of 3500 feet.  Following a cold front passage, snow levels lowered back to valley floors and post frontal showers brought up to five inches of snow to some valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was limited to elevations of 4000 feet and higher.  Local ski resorts in the zone received between eight and thirteen inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather pattern across the Eastern Pacific led to region-wide light to moderate snow.  A closed upper-level high was positioned over southeastern Alaska. The high steered modified Arctic air southward into the Inland Northwest. Meanwhile, a moist westerly jet undercut the Alaskan high bringing a source of moisture into the region. A combination of a stalled frontal boundary, topography, and isentropic processes resulted in region-wide snow. The heaviest snow was delivered in two waves; the first occurred from the evening hours of the 22nd through the evening of the 23rd with a second surge of moderate to heavy snow during the day on the 24th. The heaviest snow accumulations were concentrated across the Central Panhandle Mountains of Idaho which received between one to three feet of snow. Nearly all other valley locations received between four to ten inches over the multi-day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of heavy snow impacting the Central Panhandle Mountains of Idaho.  Snowfall amounts in the valleys ranged from eight to fourteen inches over the two day period.  In the mountains, two day totals were around two feet.  The first round of snow which fell on the 23rd dumped around a foot in the mountains and two to six inches in the valleys.  A second round of snow on the 24th brought an additional three to six inches in the valleys and foot in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist Pacific westerly flow brought snow to north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ITD reported up to 14 inches of snow had fallen along Williams Creek and locations near Salmon to Leadore reported anywhere from 5 to 8 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several schools in southeastern Idaho were closed on February 6th due to extreme cold wind chill readings of 20 below zero or colder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Falls School District, Shelley School District, Firth School District, Bonneville School District, Ririe School District, Sugar-Salem School District, and Madison School District all closed on February 6th due to wind chill readings of 20 to 35 below zero.

CZ_NAME: BIG AND LITTLE WOOD RIVER REGION
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting storm beginning after midnight on the 7th and continuing into the early morning hours on the 9th brought extremely heavy snowfall amounts to the mountains of southeast Idaho.   1 to 4 feet of snow fell at high elevations with 4 to 9 inches common in the Eastern Magic Valley and Snake River Plain before warmer temperatures moved into lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area SNOTEL reports were:  26 inches at Dollarhide, 24 inches at Chocolate Gulch, 35 inches at Galena, 34 inches at Galena Summit, 12 inches at Garfield Ranger Station, 33 inches at Lost Wood Divide, 17 inches at Hyndman, 16 inches at Swede Peak, and 48 inches at Vienna Mine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over a foot of snow fell across the higher terrain of north-central Idaho as an upper level trough laden with Pacific moisture moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MT DOT required that towing vehicles wear chains at Lolo pass Saturday evening, Jan. 11 through the morning of Jan. 12. From 8 pm MST to 12 am MST Jan. 12, the Lolo Pass SNOTEL reported 4 of heavy snow. This sensor recorded 14 total storm snowfall with 2.20 of snow water equivalent from 8 pm PST Jan. 10 through 5 am PST Jan. 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was under a transitioning weather regime through the first week of March. March 1st came in dry and very cold under a modified Arctic air mass. This kept soil temperatures below freezing across much of the central to northern Idaho Panhandle. March 2nd into the 3rd brought heavy snow across these same areas as dry northerly flow was replaced by milder and wetter southerly flow. This wetter and milder flow regime continued into March 5th, but with snow levels lifting above valleys floors. A pair of Pacific storm systems bringing through more sub-tropical moisture into the region resulted in moderate to heavy rainfall in the valleys. The combination of frozen soils, heavy rain and snow melt produced flooding across portions of the Coeur d'Alene Area and Northern Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fleming Creek came out of its banks and flooded a single home.  Damage costs were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system in the Gulf of Alaska late on March 8th through the evening of March 9th directed a slow north to south sagging atmospheric river across the area.  The origins of this atmospheric river were from off of the Islands of Hawaii.  This resulted in abnormally high moisture content over the region for early to mid-March.  This was a very mild weather pattern with precipitation falling as rain even over the highest mountain peaks.  Strong westerly flow resulted in strong orographic enhancement across the mountainous areas of the northern and central Idaho Panhandle.  This was combined with good isentropic ascent to produce moderate to heavy rainfall across the area.  Mild temperatures resulted in a significant contribution of runoff from the snow pack at mid elevations.  This resulted in high flows on the Coeur d'Alene River and along small streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration of heavy rain and snow melt resulted in high flows on the Coeur d'Alene River and surrounding small creeks and streams.  Water inundated numerous roads and low lying areas along the river from Cataldo to Harrison: Flood waters inundated CCC Road near the Mennonite Church, Latour Creek Road near a campground immediately south of Interstate 90, and along portions of Dudley Road west of Latour Creek.  High flows on Latour Creek resulted in soil erosion near the garage section of a residence on Latour Creek Road.  Damage costs were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains flooded several streets in Pocatello including Alameda.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains flooded several streets in Pocatello including Alameda.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail and gusty winds reported with thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure dropped down the Washington Coastline then swung inland into the Inland Northwest during the afternoon of July 23rd. The wave took on a strong negative tilt and moved into a moist and very unstable air mass. The outcome was a widespread severe weather event for Eastern Washington and Northern Idaho producing large hail, flash flooding, and widespread wind damage. Falling trees led to some injuries in Eastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm producing damaging winds and quarter size hail tracked through northern reaches of Shoshone County crossing Interstate 90 between Pinehurst and Kellogg.  Numerous cars at a car lot were dented from the large hail.  Trees and powerlines were knocked down in Cataldo and along Coeur D Alene Road.
